flashcards.Back: A cryptographic network protocol used for secure remote access to a device's command-line interface. Unlike Telnet, it encrypts all traffic, including credentials.
A unique name assigned to a router or switch. it is a required parameter for generating the RSA keys used by SSH.
The Cisco global command used to define the network domain for the device. it is combined with the hostname to form the FQDN for RSA key generation.
The Cisco command used to create the public and private encryption keys required for SSH communication.
The bit length of the generated encryption keys. A minimum of 768 bits is required for SSHv2, while 1024 or higher is recommended for better security.
The modern and more secure version of the SSH protocol. it is the standard for Cisco device management and addresses vulnerabilities found in SSH v1.
The global configuration command used to force a Cisco device to only support the more secure SSH v2 protocol.
The command to enter configuration mode for the virtual terminal lines, which are the logical ports used for remote network management.
A VTY line configuration command that restricts remote access to only the SSH protocol, effectively disabling insecure Telnet access.
A VTY line configuration command that instructs the device to check a user's credentials against its own local database of usernames and passwords.
A list of usernames and secret passwords stored in the device's running configuration, used by the 'login local' command for authentication.
A verification command used to confirm that the SSH server is enabled and to see which protocol version is currently running.
A verification command that displays the status of all currently active SSH client connections to the device.
The standard destination port number used for all SSH communication.
Accessing a device's management interface (like SSH) over the same network infrastructure used for standard user data traffic.
Accessing a device's management interface through a dedicated physical connection (like the console port) that is independent of the network.
A legacy management protocol that sends all data in clear text. it is highly insecure and should be avoided in production networks.
The command used to permanently delete the existing RSA keys from a device, which will also disable the SSH server.
A security setting configured via 'ip ssh timeout' that automatically disconnects an idle remote session after a certain period.
A security setting configured via 'ip ssh authentication-retries' that limits the number of failed login attempts before the session is closed.
The standard global command used to create a local user account with a highly secure, hashed password for remote access.
Fully Qualified Domain Name. The combination of hostname and domain-name (e.g., SW1.example.com) used by the RSA key generation algorithm.
An EXEC mode command often used in remote sessions to disable output paging, allowing for long configuration displays without interruptions.
Software (like PuTTY or the built-in terminal on Linux/Mac) that an administrator uses to establish an encrypted connection to a remote network device.
A legal warning message displayed to users upon connecting to a device. it is often used to discourage unauthorized management attempts.
The highest administrative access level on a Cisco device. User accounts can be configured to enter this mode automatically upon SSH login.
An ACL applied to the VTY lines (using 'access-class') to restrict SSH access to only specific authorized IP addresses or subnets.
A file transfer protocol that runs over SSH, providing a secure way to upload or download IOS images and configuration files.
A property of SSH that uses hashing to ensure that management commands have not been altered in transit between the client and the device.
A property of SSH that uses encryption to ensure that even if management traffic is intercepted, it cannot be read by an unauthorized third party.
